1: A direct revelation: "Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you." The entire Gospel of John has been oriented toward this moment by the recurring deferral — "my hour has not yet come" (John 2:4; John 7:30; John 8:20) and its anticipation (John 12:23). Now, at the threshold of the passion, the hour arrives. The prayer frames the cross as mutual glorification: the Son glorifies the Father through obedience unto death; the Father glorifies the Son through the resurrection. What looks like defeat is the supreme disclosure of who the Father and the Son are.
2: A direct revelation: "you granted him authority over all people that he might give eternal life to all those you have given him." The cosmic authority of the Son over all flesh is the Christological ground of the Gospel's universal mission. The scope is total — all flesh; the gift is the highest possible — eternal life; the recipients are those the Father has given the Son. This will become the explicit basis of the apostolic commission: "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Therefore go and make disciples of all nations" (Matthew 28:18–19).
3: A direct revelation: "Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent." The Son defines eternal life as knowledge of God-in-Christ, and within the prayer names himself as "Jesus Christ" — the full Christological title. The formula is remarkable: he prays by naming himself as the object of the faith he is calling forth. The knowledge (ginōskō) is not intellectual acquaintance but relational-participatory union — the same word used of the Father knowing the Son (John 10:15). Eternal life is not an afterlife destination but a quality of relationship with God, available now through Christ.
4: A revelation of God: "I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do." The perfect tense (eteleíōsa — I have finished/completed) declares the incarnate mission accomplished, hours before the cross. The Evangelist consistently presents the cross not as the undoing of the ministry but as its completion — its telos. The "work" given by the Father runs from the Prologue through the signs and discourses; the cross is not an interruption but the apex. The Son reports completion before it is humanly visible, because the divine perspective on the mission is that it is already done.
5: A direct revelation: "And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began." The Son prays for the restoration of his pre-incarnate glory — "the glory I had," speaking from personal experience of a state before creation. This is the theological claim of the Prologue (John 1:1–2) now spoken as prayer: the incarnation was a voluntary entry into the creaturely sphere, and the resurrection-exaltation is the return to the eternal state. The "before the world began" (pro katabolēs kosmou) is the anchor of John's high Christology — the Son existed before and will exist beyond creation.
6: A revelation of God: "I have revealed you to those whom you gave me out of the world." The Son's mission as the revealer of the Father — announced in the Prologue (John 1:18 — "he has made him known") — is here reported as completed. "I have manifested your name" (literal rendering of the Greek ephanerōsa) means the character, identity, and covenant faithfulness of the Father have been disclosed through the Son's person and work. The disciples are described as the Father's gift to the Son — a motif that recurs throughout this prayer (vv 2, 9, 24) and grounds the Son's intercession on their behalf.
7: A revelation of God: "Now they know that everything you have given me comes from you." The disciples' theological understanding — that the Son is the Father's representative, and that every word and work of the Son originates in the Father — is the fruit of the incarnate mission. Their knowledge is still partial; the full comprehension awaits the resurrection and the Spirit (John 14:26; John 16:13). But the foundation is laid: they have received the Son as sent by the Father, which is the essential content of the faith the Gospel calls for.
8: A direct revelation: "For I gave them the words you gave me and they accepted them. They knew with certainty that I came from you, and they believed that you sent me." The chain of divine communication — Father → Son → disciples — is the structure of all revelation in John. Christ's words are not his own (John 14:24 — "the words you hear are not my own; they belong to the Father who sent me") but are the Father's transmission through the Son. The disciples' faith — that Jesus came from God and was sent by God — is the core Christological confession the Gospel asks of every reader.
9: A revelation of God: "I pray for them. I am not praying for the world, but for those you have given me, for they are yours." The narrowing of the intercession to the disciples is not a denial of the Son's love for the world (John 3:16) but a specification of this prayer's scope. The disciples are the ones through whom the world will receive the Gospel (v20 — Jesus will pray for future believers through the disciples' message). The priestly intercession is particular before it is universal: the Son prays first for those who will carry the word to the rest.
10: A direct revelation: "All I have is yours, and all you have is mine. And glory has come to me through them." The mutual possessing of all things between Father and Son is the Christological statement of the Son's full divinity: to say "all you have is mine" is to claim co-equality with the Father. The glory coming to the Son through the disciples is the fruit of mission — their existence as believers is already the glorification of the one who called them. The mission to the disciples has been the Father's glory expressed through the Son's work.
11: A direct revelation: "Holy Father, protect them by the power of your name, the name you gave me, so that they may be one as we are one." The address "Holy Father" is unique in the NT — the divine holiness grounds the appeal for the disciples' protection. The name given to the Son (the divine name, which the Son has borne and revealed throughout John — John 17:6; ego eimi in John 8:58; John 18:5–6) is the power that guards them. The unity prayed for — "as we are one" — takes the Trinitarian unity as both the model and the source of the church's unity.
12: A direct fulfillment: "None has been lost except the one doomed to destruction so that Scripture would be fulfilled." The protection of the disciples during the earthly ministry is complete; Judas's loss is framed not as failure but as scriptural anticipation (Psalms 41:9, cited in John 13:18; cf. Psalms 109:8 applied to Judas in Acts 1:20). The Evangelist consistently shows that every element of the passion — including the betrayal — was not an interruption of the Father's plan but woven into it before the foundation of the world. The exception confirms the rule: divine protection operates according to divine wisdom, not human calculation.
13: A revelation of God: "I say these things while I am still in the world, so that they may have the full measure of my joy within them." The joy of Christ — rooted in his unbroken communion with the Father — is offered as the disciples' inheritance. The departing Son does not leave the disciples in fear; he leaves them in his joy, which the Spirit will sustain (John 15:11; John 16:22–24). The cross that is hours away is approached in joy, not despair, because the Son sees beyond it to the resurrection and the homecoming to the Father's presence (v5). That joy is transferable to all who are united to him.
14: A revelation of God: "I have given them your word and the world has hated them, for they are not of the world any more than I am of the world." The disciples' alienation from the world is derived from and modeled on Christ's alienation from the world. The pattern of rejection that Jesus experienced (John 1:11 — "he came to his own, and his own did not receive him") is extended to the community of the Son. Receiving the divine word sets the disciples apart from the world's value system — not by religious separation but by the transformation that comes from hearing a different word than the world hears.
15: A revelation of God: "My prayer is not that you take them out of the world but that you protect them from the evil one." The Son does not pray for his followers' extraction from the dangerous world but for their protection within it. They are left in the world on mission (v18). The "evil one" (ponēros) — the power behind Judas's betrayal (John 13:2, 27) and the world's opposition — is a real adversary; the prayer for protection acknowledges the threat while asserting the Father's power over it. The disciples' calling is presence-in-the-world, not escape from it.
16: A revelation of God: "They are not of the world, even as I am not of it." The disciples' not-belonging-to-the-world is derived from and shares in Christ's not-belonging. In John, the disciples' identity is consistently defined by their union with the Son: they are what he is; they stand where he stands; they face what he faced. The Christological grounding of the church's distinct identity in the world is not a sociological description but a participation in the Son's own relationship to the created order — he is in the world but not sourced by it; they share that status by union with him.
17: A direct revelation: "Sanctify them by the truth; your word is truth." The consecration (hagiazō) of the disciples through the divine word is the priestly application of the truth-theme that pervades John (John 8:32; John 14:6). Since the Evangelist identifies Jesus as "the truth" (John 14:6), the prayer that the disciples be sanctified by truth is also a prayer that they be formed by and into Christ. The word that sanctifies is both the Scripture and the Son — the written word and the incarnate Word are inseparable instruments of the disciples' consecration.
18: A direct revelation: "As you sent me into the world, I have sent them into the world." The apostolic mission of the disciples derives from and mirrors the mission of the Son. The Son is the apostellō — the Sent One whose identity in John is inseparable from his being sent by the Father; now he himself sends, extending the Father's mission through him to the world through the disciples. The Great Commission (Matthew 28:18–20) is the explicit command form of what is here stated as prayer. The disciples' mission is the continuation and expression of the Son's own mission.
19: A direct revelation: "For them I sanctify myself, that they too may be truly sanctified." The key verse of the High Priestly Prayer: hagiazō emauton — "I sanctify/consecrate myself." The verb hagiazō is the LXX term for consecrating priests (Exodus 28:41) and setting apart sacrificial offerings (Exodus 13:2; Deuteronomy 15:19). The Son consecrates himself to the cross as simultaneously the priest who offers and the sacrifice offered. His self-consecration to death is the ground on which the disciples' sanctification rests: they are made holy because he made himself holy for them. This single verse is the exegetical basis for calling John 17 the High Priestly Prayer — the priest consecrates the sacrifice, and the sacrifice is himself.
20: A revelation of God: "My prayer is not for them alone. I pray also for those who will believe in me through their message." The prayer extends beyond the Eleven to all future believers — including those reading the Gospel. The Evangelist places the reader within the scope of the Son's intercession: John 17 is a prayer for every disciple who will come through the chain of apostolic witness. The church existed first in prayer before it existed in history. The Son's intercession for the not-yet-believing is the grace that undergirds the proclamation that will call them forth.
21: A direct revelation: "that all of them may be one, Father, just as you are in me and I am in you. May they also be in us so that the world may believe that you have sent me." The unity for which the Son prays is defined by Trinitarian unity — the mutual indwelling of Father and Son (John 10:38; John 14:10–11) is both the model and the basis for the community's unity. The purpose of unity is missional: so the world may believe the Son was sent. The fractured state of visible Christianity is the failure of the conditions this prayer requires; the prayer itself stands as the theological norm and the church's ongoing calling.
22: A direct revelation: "I have given them the glory that you gave me, that they may be one as we are one." The glory given to the disciples is the shared relational life that belongs to those who are in Christ — not the divine nature itself but the participatory union with the Father that the Son possesses and shares. This is the Christological basis for what Eastern theology calls theosis: the believers' participation in the divine life through union with the Son. The gift of glory is not an external reward but an interior sharing in what the Son has always had with the Father.
23: A direct revelation: "I in them and you in me — so that they may be brought to complete unity. Then the world will know that you sent me and have loved them even as you have loved me." The chain of mutual indwelling — Father in Son, Son in disciples — is the structure of the new covenant community. The climactic claim is that the Father loves the disciples with the same love with which he loves the eternal Son. The love the Father has for the pre-existent Son (v24 — "because you loved me before the creation of the world") is extended to all who are united to the Son. This is the highest assertion of the believer's standing before God in the NT.
24: A direct revelation: "Father, I want those you have given me to be with me where I am, and to see my glory, the glory you have given me because you loved me before the creation of the world." The Son's final petition is eschatological: the disciples' ultimate destiny is to see his full unveiled glory. The love of the Father for the Son before creation (pro katabolēs kosmou) is the eternal foundation of everything — the glory, the mission, the disciples' hope. The Beatific Vision — the face-to-face sight of the Son in his full glory — is what the disciples are headed toward. The prayer anchors their hope in the Father's pre-creation love.
25: A revelation of God: "Righteous Father, though the world does not know you, I know you, and they know that you have sent me." The final address — "Righteous Father" — is the appeal to the divine character that ensures vindication of the Son and protection of the disciples. The righteousness of the Father will be demonstrated in the resurrection of the one the world condemned. The contrast between the world's ignorance and the disciples' knowledge is the fruit of the Son's revelatory mission (John 1:18 — "no one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son... has made him known"). The Son alone bridges the gap between the unknowing world and the knowing Father.
26: A direct revelation: "I have made you known to them, and will continue to make you known in order that the love you have for me may be in them and that I myself may be in them." The ongoing revelation of the Father through the Son — "will continue to make you known" — is the ministry the Spirit will carry forward (John 14:26; John 16:13–15). The purpose of continued revelation is the inhabitation of the disciples by the divine love and by the Son himself. The prayer's final clause — "that I myself may be in them" — is the Evangelist's ultimate Christological statement: the Son abides in disciples as the Father abides in the Son (John 14:10). The prayer closes where the Gospel will end: the Son present in his people.
